TN.pre_k_program: Voluntary Pre-K (VPK) — competitive state grant administered by TDOE; districts apply annually
TN.literacy_initiative: Tennessee Reads / TN ELI / Reading 360 (2021 Literacy Success Act) — driving K-3 curriculum reviews
TN.leadership_terminology: Districts use "Director of Schools" instead of "Superintendent"
notes: TN-VPK is a competitive state grant administered by TDOE; districts apply annually. Funding is capped and serves ~18K four-year-olds statewide — a small fraction of eligible kids.
"Director of Schools" is Tennessee's legal title for Superintendent. For districts under ~500–1,000 students, the Director of Schools often owns ECE decisions directly.

In small/mid-size TN districts without a dedicated ECE role, the Federal Programs Director or Title I Director is the most common ECE decision-maker (since VPK funding flows through Title I/federal channels).
Tennessee Reads / TN ELI / Reading 360 (post-2021 Literacy Success Act) has driven district-wide K-3 curriculum reviews — a strong adjacent signal for ECE outreach.

Tennessee has independent city school systems within counties — known as "Special School Districts" (SSDs). Examples: Lenoir City, Maryville, Bristol, Kingsport, FSSD/Franklin Special, Bartlett, Collierville, Germantown.
Many smaller TN districts blend Pre-K with Head Start via partnerships with local Family Resource Agencies (e.g., Bradley, Smith, Johnson Counties).
Memphis-Shelby County Schools (MSCS) ended its Porter-Leath PreK partnership in 2021 and now operates Pre-K directly through district staff.
Sullivan County Schools announced a major PreK expansion in April 2026 — adding PreK to all 10 elementary schools via a federal grant. Strong outreach signal.
Dickson County Schools was the first TN district (~2023) to create a dedicated "Director of Early Learning" role — a model others may follow.
Murfreesboro City Schools just opened a stand-alone Cason Lane Pre-K building — fresh adoption window for a major district.
Putnam County's Diana Wood was named TDOE 2025-26 Supervisor of the Year — strong relationship signal.
TN district email domains vary widely: *.k12tn.net (Knox, Monroe, Bedford, Carter), *.k12.tn.us (Cocke, Hardin), branded .org / .com / .edu (Williamson, MNPS, MSCS, Bristol, FSSD).

SD.pre_k_program: No universal state Pre-K. Most districts run PreK via Title I funds (federal) or partner with Head Start agencies. Some district-funded programs at larger systems.
SD.leadership_terminology: Most SD districts are very small (<500 students); Superintendents commonly hold multiple roles (Supt + Curriculum + Title I + Special Ed)
notes: South Dakota has no universal state Pre-K program. PreK in SD is typically funded via federal Title I, federal Head Start (separate agency, not district staff), or local district funding. Most districts under ~500 students have no dedicated ECE role.
SD district email convention is overwhelmingly `firstname.lastname@k12.sd.us` (centrally hosted by SD DOE). A few districts use branded domains (e.g., Spearfish, Rapid City, Sioux Falls).
The SD DOE Public Schools Directory is the canonical staff source — searchable via doe.sd.gov.

Tribal/BIE districts on reservations (Pine Ridge, Rosebud, Cheyenne River, Standing Rock) follow separate federal funding/structure. Public districts adjacent to reservations may serve mixed populations.
Sioux Falls School District 49-5 (~24K students) is the only SD district with a clearly dedicated Director of Early Learning / Birth-to-Three Supervisor (Jennifer A. Bauer).

Largest SD districts with dedicated Curriculum Director persona: Aberdeen, Brandon Valley, Rapid City, Mitchell, Harrisburg, Brookings, Watertown, Yankton, Pierre, Huron, Tea Area, West Central, Spearfish, Madison Central, Mobridge-Pollock.
Most small/rural districts default to Superintendent as the ECE decision-maker — many wear multiple hats.
Yankton is building a 73K sq ft Early Childhood Development Center — strong outreach signal.
Recent leadership transitions to verify before outreach: Stanley County (Coverdale incoming Jan 2026), Menno (search ongoing), Castlewood (possible Books transition), Bowdle, Eagle Butte, Garretson.

ND.pre_k_program: No large state Pre-K. ND has the "Best in Class" Early Childhood Education Grant (passed 2021) for 4-year-olds — a small competitive grant. Most PreK runs via federal Title I or Head Start partnerships.
ND.leadership_terminology: ND districts are predominantly very small Plains rural districts; Superintendents typically own ECE decisions directly.
notes: ND has no large state Pre-K program. The "Best in Class" Early Childhood Education Grant (passed 2021) provides modest competitive funding for 4-year-olds. PreK is mainly funded via federal Title I or Head Start.

ND district email convention is overwhelmingly `firstname.lastname@k12.nd.us` (centrally hosted by NDDPI). Branded-domain exceptions: Bismarck (bismarck.k12.nd.us is special), Mandan (msd1.org), Williston Basin (willistonschools.org), Dickinson (dpsnd.org), Grand Forks (gfschools.org), Minot (minotpublicschools.org), West Fargo (westfargoschools.org), Fargo (fargo.k12.nd.us), Thompson (tps-k12.org), Mapleton (mapletonfalcons.org), Emerado (emeradok8.com), New England (ndnewengland.com), Finley-Sharon (finleysharonschool.com).
NDDPI (Department of Public Instruction) and Regional Education Associations (REAs) are state/regional — NOT district staff.

Tribal/BIE districts on reservations follow separate federal funding. Reservation-adjacent public districts include New Town, Parshall, St. John, Fort Yates, Mandaree, Solen, Twin Buttes — these may serve mixed populations.

Largest ND districts (Bismarck, Fargo, West Fargo, Grand Forks, Minot, Dickinson, Mandan) have dedicated Curriculum Directors or Asst. Supts for Teaching & Learning. Bismarck runs ECE through BECEP under Special Services. Most other districts have Superintendent as the ECE decision-maker.

Standout ECE-aligned contacts: Bismarck (Brad Barnhardt, Asst Supt Elementary), Minot (Todd Kaylor, Curriculum), West Fargo (Heather Sand, Director of Curriculum & Instruction), Dickinson (Melanie Kathrein), Fargo (Dr. Robert Grosz, Associate Supt for Teaching & Learning), McKenzie County 1 / Watford City (Calina Krogen, Director of Teaching and Learning).
Several districts had leadership transitions to verify before outreach: Surrey (mid-transition), Grand Forks (current leadership transition).
Closed/dormant districts to skip: EARL 18 / Squaw Gap (closed); Marmarth (14 students); Roosevelt 18 / Twin Buttes 37 (no current public-facing staff).

NH.pre_k_program: No state-funded universal Pre-K. NH ranks among the lowest states in state PreK access. Districts run PreK via Title I or local funds only.
NH.leadership_terminology: NH organizes districts by School Administrative Unit (SAU) — ~80 SAUs cover the state. Many small towns share an SAU central office; a few SAUs cover 5+ towns.
notes: NH has no state-funded universal Pre-K. PreK in NH runs via federal Title I, local district funding, or Special Education preschool programs. NH ranks among the lowest states for state PreK access.

NH district structure: ~80 School Administrative Units (SAUs) act as the central office for one or more towns. A "School District" is the per-town entity but staff sit at the SAU. Small towns commonly share an SAU (e.g., SAU 9 covers Albany/Bartlett/Chatham/Conway/Eaton/Freedom/Hart's Location/Jackson/Madison/Tamworth).

For most NH districts under ~1,500 students, the **Superintendent** is the right ECE decision-maker. Larger districts (Manchester, Nashua, Concord, Rochester, Dover, Portsmouth, Salem, Bedford, Keene, Merrimack, Hudson, Lebanon) have dedicated Curriculum Directors or PreK Coordinators.

Standout dedicated Pre-K/ECE leads: Manchester, Nashua (Caitlin Burns, NEEP Coordinator), Concord (Sue Carignan, Preschool Coordinator), Portsmouth (Kristen Heath, Pre-K Coordinator), Keene (Joanne Mulligan, Jonathan Daniels Preschool), Lebanon (Karen DuBois, Director of Academics), SAU 16 / Exeter (Karen Ringuette, PreK Coordinator), SAU 47 / Jaffrey-Rindge (Storm Medders), SAU 15 / Hooksett (Rebecca Roy), Timberlane (Kim Rivers, Early Childhood Coordinator), Hampton Falls/N. Hampton (Shay Cassily, Director of Curriculum).
Special Education Director is a common alternative ECE persona in NH small districts — preschool is often housed under SpEd because Part B/619 federal funds support it.

Email convention: most districts use `sau##.org` or `sau##.us` (SAU number domains); larger districts use branded `mansd.org`, `nashua.edu`, `dover.k12.nh.us`, `keene.k12.nh.us`, `rochesterschools.com`, etc.
NHED (NH Department of Education) and Community Action / Head Start agencies (Strafford CAP, Belknap-Merrimack, etc.) are NOT district staff and are excluded from the dashboard.

Active leadership transitions to verify before outreach (as of 2025-26): SAU 6 Claremont (Broadrick incoming), SAU 50 Greenland/Newington (Zadravec retiring), SAU 53 Pembroke (Sherman retiring), SAU 32 (Leggett possibly relocating to CT), SAU 58 (Landry leaving for MA), SAU 76 Lyme (active search), SAU 60 Fall Mountain (transition), SAU 109 (Unity splitting July 2026), Rochester (Fulgoni interim→permanent).

MS.pre_k_program: Early Learning Collaborative (ELC) Act of 2013 — voluntary state Pre-K administered by MDE Office of Early Childhood. ELCs are public-private partnerships of districts + Head Start + private childcare. Coverage limited to ~30% of eligible 4-year-olds.
MS.leadership_terminology: Mix of county-wide districts (DeSoto, Madison, Rankin, Lafayette, Hinds, Harrison, Jackson) and city/municipal districts (Jackson Public, Hattiesburg, Tupelo, Oxford, Gulfport).
notes: MS has the **Early Learning Collaborative (ELC) Act of 2013** — voluntary state Pre-K, MDE-administered. ELCs are partnerships of districts + Head Start + private childcare. Districts in an ELC have an "ELC Lead Partner" — strong outreach contact.

Confirmed ELC participants worth prioritizing: Cleveland (Patsy Clerk), Mississippi Achievement (Sametra Brown), Pass Christian (Mandy Lacy), Starkville-Oktibbeha (Ellen Goodman, SOELC Program Manager), Rankin County (Lakeisha Maxer), Greenwood-Leflore, Jefferson County, Hattiesburg, Lowndes County, Pascagoula-Gautier, Prentiss County, South Panola, Marion County, West Point.

Standout ECE-specific contacts: Pearl (Stacy Farrar, ECEC Director), Laurel (Marlene Jackson, Early Childhood Coordinator), Hinds County (Smith-Angrum, ECE Curriculum Director), Lee County (Jodie Boyd, ECE Director), Pascagoula-Gautier (Kelli McCorkle, Early Beginnings Director — 2024 ELC grant signal), Petal (Jana Perry, Coleman Center), South Tippah (Angie Gossett, Pre-K Director), Canton (Shoney Anderson-Harris, Director of Literacy & ECE), Hattiesburg (Hope Mikell, PreK-5 Curriculum), Jackson (Felicia Thomas, Director of Early Childhood).

For small rural MS districts with no dedicated ECE staff, the **Federal Programs Director / Title I Director** is the right persona — Title I typically funds preschool there. Superintendent is the fallback for tiniest districts (<500 students).

Email convention varies widely. Most districts use `*.k12.ms.us` or branded `.org`. Several districts use staff email domains different from their public website (Lauderdale = lced.net, Tate = tcsdms.org, Franklin = fcsd.k12.ms.us, East Tallahatchie = etsdk12.org, Forrest County, Marshall County, Sunflower County, West Tallahatchie).

MDE (Mississippi Department of Education), Excel by Five (non-profit), and Head Start grantees (MS Action for Community Education, Friends of Children of Mississippi) are NOT district staff and are excluded.

Districts under MDE state takeover or in transition (verify before outreach): Okolona (Nov 2025 takeover), Wilkinson (Jan 2026 takeover), Greenwood-Leflore (Mary S. Johnson recently promoted Supt), West Tallahatchie, Forrest County, Amory.
Districts to deprioritize: Forrest County Agricultural HS (grades 9-12 only, no ECE).
Buying signals to act on: Moss Point received a $900K Pre-K grant; Pascagoula-Gautier received a 2024 ELC grant; West Point is Excel by 5 certified.

KY.pre_k_program: State-funded Pre-K for 4-year-olds at 160% of federal poverty level + 3-4 year olds with disabilities. Administered by KY Department of Education (KDE). Most districts have a Pre-K Coordinator or Director of Early Learning.
KY.literacy_initiative: Read to Succeed Act / Senate Bill 9 (2022) — drove a major K-3 literacy overhaul (KyMTSS, Heggerty, etc.). Strong adjacent signal for ECE outreach.
KY.leadership_terminology: Mix of county districts (e.g., JCPS / Jefferson County, Fayette / Lexington, Boone, Kenton, Warren) and city/independent districts (Anchorage, Ashland, Beechwood, Bowling Green, Berea, Erlanger-Elsmere, etc.). Independent districts are city-only and exist within a county that has its own separate district.
notes: KY has state-funded Pre-K for 4-year-olds at 160% federal poverty level + 3-4 year olds with disabilities, administered by KDE. Most districts have a dedicated Pre-K Coordinator or Director of Early Learning.
Read to Succeed Act / Senate Bill 9 (2022) drove a major K-3 literacy overhaul (KyMTSS, Heggerty) — this is a strong adjacent signal for ECE outreach.

KY districts split into ~120 county districts and ~50 city/independent districts. Independent districts exist *within* a county that already has its own separate district. Tiny independents (Anchorage, Burgin, Pineville, Science Hill, Southgate, East Bernstadt, Fulton, Williamstown, Paintsville) typically have only a Superintendent as the ECE contact.

A common KY pattern at small/mid districts: combined "Director of Special Education / Preschool" role (because federal Part B/619 funds support 3-4yo with disabilities). Examples: Boyle (Chelsea Clark), Lincoln (Julie Ball), Trimble (Alicia Jackson), Owen (Shonna Gullett).

Standout dedicated ECE/Preschool Coordinators: Erlanger-Elsmere (Jessica Soules), Floyd (Kimberly Grubb, Chief Early Childhood Officer), Hardin (Carlena Sheeran), Washington (Paula Turner), Robertson (Tamara Leadingham), Bowling Green (Amy Sample), Henderson (PreK expansion 2026-27), Hopkins (Clarissa Byard), Pike (Misty Riddle).

Email convention: most KY districts use `[name].kyschools.us` (e.g., `jefferson.kyschools.us`, `fayette.kyschools.us`). A few small districts have website on `.k12.ky.us` while staff email runs on kyschools.us (Breathitt, Hickman, Jenkins, Nicholas).

KDE (Kentucky Department of Education), regional cooperatives (West Kentucky Educational Cooperative, GRREC, KEDC), and Community Action / Head Start agencies are NOT district staff and are excluded from the dashboard.

Buying signals to act on: Henderson County preschool expansion 2026-27, Nelson County opened new Early Learning Center, Paris/TBJELC achieved Level 5 KY All STARS, Harlan County preschool investment, Floyd County named Chief Early Childhood Officer (Kimberly Grubb).
Largest KY districts: Jefferson County (JCPS, ~96K), Fayette (~42K), Boone, Kenton, Warren, Hardin, Daviess, Madison, Bullitt — all have dedicated ECE leadership.

AK.pre_k_program: Limited state-funded Pre-K. The Alaska Reads Act (2022) mandated K-3 literacy improvements with optional Pre-K provisions but funding is modest. Most PreK runs via federal Title I, federal Head Start, Tribal/BIE programs, or local district funding.
AK.literacy_initiative: Alaska Reads Act (2022) — K-3 literacy mandate driving curriculum reviews; strong adjacent signal for ECE outreach.
AK.leadership_terminology: Alaska has 54 districts: 19 Borough School Districts (organized boroughs), 19 Regional Education Attendance Areas (REAAs covering unorganized boroughs), Anchorage School District (largest), plus city/independent districts (Sitka, Petersburg, Fairbanks North Star, etc.).
notes: Alaska has 54 districts with three flavors: Borough School Districts (organized boroughs), REAAs (unorganized borough regions covering many remote villages), and city/independent districts. Anchorage is the largest (~43K students); Mat-Su, Fairbanks North Star, and Juneau are next.

Alaska Reads Act (2022) mandated K-3 literacy improvements with optional Pre-K provisions. Funding is modest, so most AK PreK runs via federal Title I, federal Head Start (RurAL CAP is the main grantee), Tribal/BIE programs, or local district programs.
Many REAAs serve 10-50 schools across vast geographic areas (e.g., Bering Strait, Lower Kuskokwim, NW Arctic Borough, Yukon-Koyukuk). For these, the Superintendent is the right ECE persona.

Standout dedicated ECE/Curriculum leads: Anchorage (Jessica Nesset, Director of Early Learning), Fairbanks North Star (Chane Beam, Exec Director Teaching & Learning), Mat-Su (Beckie Murphy), Juneau (Kristin Garot), Kodiak (Kim Saunders, Director of Special Services overseeing ECE), NW Arctic (Wanda Baltazar), Lower Kuskokwim (Alicia Miner), Kenai (Eric Pederson), Sitka (Diana Fulton), Ketchikan (Alonso Escalante), Kuspuk (Emily Vanderpool), Alaska Gateway (Suzanne Pascuzzi, Federal Programs).

Email convention varies widely: Anchorage uses `last_first@asdk12.org`, Fairbanks/Juneau/NSBSD use `first.last`, Mat-Su uses `firstname.lastname@matsuk12.us`, Kodiak uses `First.Last@kibsd.org`, Sitka uses `lastfirstinitial@ssd.org`. Some districts use `*.k12.ak.us` legacy domains.

Alaska DEED (Department of Education and Early Development) is state-level — NOT recorded as district staff. RurAL CAP (Rural Alaska Community Action Program) is a Head Start grantee — also NOT district staff.

NM.pre_k_program: NM PreK Program — strong state-funded Pre-K administered by the Early Childhood Education and Care Department (ECECD) for 3-4 year olds. Mixed-Delivery model partners districts with private childcare and Head Start.
NM.literacy_initiative: K-5+ Plus (extended day) and a K-3 literacy initiative.
NM.leadership_terminology: Districts vary widely — Albuquerque (largest, ~73K) and other large districts (Las Cruces, Rio Rancho, Santa Fe, Gadsden, Hobbs, Farmington) have dedicated ECE staff. Many small/rural districts (especially in tribal/Pueblo areas, Navajo Nation) have limited staff.
notes: NM has the **NM PreK Program** administered by the Early Childhood Education and Care Department (ECECD) — a robust state-funded Pre-K program for 3-4 year olds. The Mixed-Delivery model partners districts with private childcare providers and Head Start.
NM also has K-5+ Plus (extended day) and a K-3 literacy initiative.
NMPED (NM Public Education Department), ECECD (Early Childhood Education and Care Department), and Tribal Head Start agencies are NOT district staff and are excluded.
NM has Bureau of Indian Education (BIE) districts on Pueblo and Navajo Nation lands — separate federal structure.

Standout NM ECE leads (existing in dashboard): Cathy Chavez (Los Lunas), Molly Pierce, John Maldonado, Kimberly Ortiz (Magdalena — note: on admin leave, resigning 6/30/2026), Curtis Clough, Edward Fluhman (Roy Municipal — agent flagged replacement: Dr. Jim Holloway is now in role).

Standout new finds: Albuquerque (large), Las Cruces (large), Rio Rancho, Santa Fe, Gadsden, Hobbs (Amy Rhoads), Española (Holly Martinez), Las Vegas City (Cathy Gallegos), Raton (Kristie Medina), Grants-Cibola (Desiraye Lopez), Hatch Valley (Michael Chavez), Belen (Lawrence Sanchez), Mora (Norma Cavazos — replaced interim Trujillo), Dora (Brandon Hays), Clayton (Stacy Diller), Los Alamos (Gina Terrazas, PreK Director).

Email convention: most districts use branded `.org` or `.k12.nm.us` (`aps.edu` for Albuquerque, `lcps.net` for Las Cruces, `rrps.net` for Rio Rancho, `sfps.info` for Santa Fe, `gisd.us` for Gadsden, `hobbsschools.net`).

Active leadership transitions to verify before outreach: Hagerman (Williams replaced by Belia Reyes), Mora (Trujillo→Cavazos), Logan (Schofield was Utah misattribution; NM Logan now interim Ross Perschbacher), Tucumcari (Johnson retired June 2025 → Deanne McKinney), Magdalena (Kim Ortiz on admin leave, resigning 6/30/2026), Truth or Consequences + Cobre (Randall Piper concurrently serves both as Superintendent / Interim Supt for 25-26 SY).
Note: ids 595 and 17661 in our data are duplicates — both represent Truth or Consequences Schools.

AR.pre_k_program: Arkansas Better Chance (ABC) — state-funded Pre-K administered by DESE for 3-4 year olds at 200% federal poverty level. Many districts have a dedicated ABC Director or Pre-K Coordinator.
AR.literacy_initiative: LEARNS Act (2023) — major K-12 reform package: voucher expansion, literacy mandates, teacher pay raises. Strong adjacent signal for ECE outreach.
AR.leadership_terminology: Arkansas has ~233 districts as a mix of county and city/town districts. Largest: Little Rock, Fort Smith, Springdale, Bentonville, Rogers, Conway, North Little Rock, Pulaski County Special, Cabot, Fayetteville.
notes: AR has the **Arkansas Better Chance (ABC)** state-funded Pre-K program for 3-4 year olds at 200% FPL, administered by DESE (Division of Elementary and Secondary Education). Many districts have a dedicated ABC Director or Pre-K Coordinator.
The **LEARNS Act (2023)** was a major K-12 reform package — voucher expansion, K-3 literacy mandates, teacher pay raises. Strong adjacent signal for ECE/curriculum outreach.

AR has 15 Educational Service Cooperatives (ESCs) — Arch Ford, NW AR ESC, etc. Some districts route ECE through their ESC. ESC staff are NOT recorded as district contacts unless they are explicitly embedded at a host district.

Standout dedicated ECE/ABC leads: Little Rock (Esther Jones, Director of Early Childhood Programs), North Little Rock (Holly Moore, ECE Coordinator), Cabot (Suzie Kelley, Director of Pre-K), Conway (Heather Leavell, Sallie Cone Preschool), Pine Bluff (Marceinia Peoples, Pre-K Administrator), Bentonville (Lori Knight, Asst Director ECE), Fort Smith (Sarah Yancey, ECE Supervisor), Bryant (Tisha Long, ECE Director), Texarkana (Dana Walker, ABC Director), Van Buren (Robert Childers, ABC Coordinator), Springdale (Brandy Wolfenbarger, ECC Director), Paragould (Vicki Shelby, Director of ECE), Concord (Meagan Spinks, ABC Director), Harrison (Jeff Winkle, Director of ECE), Hermitage (Jade Huitt, Pre-K), Greenbrier (Angela Betancourt, ABCSS Director), Jonesboro (Allison Clem, PreK Director), Lake Hamilton (Renae Carrouth, Pre-K Coordinator).
For small AR districts (<500 students), Superintendent or combined Federal Programs/Curriculum Director is the default ECE persona — many small AR districts have admins wearing multiple hats.
Email convention: most districts use branded `.org` or `.k12.ar.us` (e.g., `lrsd.org`, `fortsmithschools.org`, `sdale.org`, `bentonvillek12.org`, `rps.k12.ar.us`, `conwayschools.org`).
DESE / ADE (Arkansas Department of Education), the 15 Educational Service Cooperatives, and Head Start grantees are NOT district staff and are excluded from the dashboard.

Districts under state monitoring/takeover to verify before outreach: Marvell-Elaine (state takeover 2023, ongoing). Several districts had recent supt transitions where names couldn't be verified: Bearden, Clarendon, Dierks, Fordyce, West Memphis, Blevins, Cossatot River, Mount Ida, Searcy County.

NE.pre_k_program: Sixpence Early Learning Fund (birth-3) + Early Childhood Education Grant Program (4-year-olds), administered by NDE Office of Early Childhood. Moderate funding; districts often partner with community providers.
NE.leadership_terminology: Nebraska is dominated by very small Class III/IV/V/VI districts. Largest: Omaha Public Schools (OPS, ~52K), Lincoln Public Schools (LPS, ~42K), Millard, Westside, Papillion-La Vista, Bellevue, Elkhorn, Grand Island, Kearney, North Platte, Fremont.
notes: NE has two state-funded ECE programs: **Sixpence Early Learning Fund** (birth to 3) and the **Early Childhood Education Grant Program** (4-year-olds). Both administered by the Nebraska Department of Education (NDE) Office of Early Childhood. Funding is moderate; districts often partner with community providers.

Standout dedicated ECE leads: Omaha Public Schools (Jorja Brazda-Witters, Early Childhood Coordinator), Lincoln Public Schools (Cara Lucas-Richt, Director of Early Childhood), Grand Island (Elizabeth Lantz, Director of ECE), Hastings (Kerra Robinson, Morton Early Learning Center), Lexington (Tracy Naylor, Early Learning Academy Director), Kearney (Jenn True, PK-5 Director), Elkhorn (Tracie Burton, ECE Coordinator), Scottsbluff (Bree Rock, Bear Cub Preschool), Beatrice (Missy Timmerman, Community Preschool Director), Wayne (Misty Beair, ELC Director), Holdrege (Shana Orr), Plattsmouth (Juli Beck), South Sioux City (Dr. Rony Ortega — also on NE Board of Early Childhood Education Endowment, hot target).
For most NE districts (especially Class III/IV/V/VI rural districts <500 students), the **Superintendent** is the right ECE persona. Many small NE districts have admins wearing multiple hats.

NE has 17 Educational Service Units (ESUs) — regional service agencies that provide shared services. Some districts route ECE through their ESU. ESU staff are NOT recorded as district contacts unless explicitly embedded at a host district.

Email convention: most use branded `.org` (`ops.org`, `lps.org`, `mpsomaha.org`, `westside66.net`, `paplv.org`, `bps.org`, `elkhornweb.org`, `gips.org`, `kearneypublicschools.org`, `nppsd.org`, `fpsne.org`). Many small districts use mascot-themed domains that differ from their public website (apsbulldogs.org, dtcardinals.org, hpstigers.org, kpslonghorns.org, vcsbadger.net, wrrsd.org). Some small districts use ESU-hosted email (esu1.org, esu2.org, esu7.org).
NDE (Nebraska Department of Education) is state-level — NOT recorded as district staff. The 17 ESUs are regional and excluded.
Leadership transitions to verify before outreach: Red Cloud (active transition Dec 2025).
NDE publishes a public superintendent CSV — high-leverage backfill source for any name-only entries that need email enrichment.

ME.pre_k_program: Public Pre-K — universal optional 4-year-old program administered by Maine DOE. The state has been actively expanding Pre-K access. Many districts have a Pre-K Coordinator.
ME.leadership_terminology: Maine's school structure is unique: MSAD (Maine School Administrative Districts), RSU (Regional School Units, post-2007 reorganization), AOS (Alternative Organizational Structures — collaborative central-office sharing), CSD (Community School Districts), School Unions, and standalone "Public Schools" / "School Department" entities. Many small towns share a Superintendent via AOS/Union structures.
notes: Maine has 5 district structures: **MSAD** (older, e.g. MSAD 1, 51), **RSU** (post-2007, e.g. RSU 5, 21), **AOS** (collaborative central-office sharing without full consolidation, e.g. AOS 91, 96), **CSD** (Community School Districts), **School Unions** (older multi-town consortia), and standalone single-municipality "Public Schools" / "School Department".

AOS sharing is huge in Maine — many "Public Schools" entries are member towns sharing a single Superintendent. Examples: AOS 96 (East Machias/Marshfield/Northfield/Cutler/Machias share Nicole Case), AOS 77 (Alexander/Crawford/Pembroke/Lubec share Mary Ellen Day), AOS 91/MDIRSS (Mount Desert/Tremont/Southwest Harbor/Cranberry Isles share Michael Zboray), AOS 90 (Baileyville/Meddybemps/Carroll Plt/MSAD 30 share Amanda Belanger), VUESC (Madawaska/MSAD 33 share Benjamin Sirois). Cluster districts will share contact info — that is correct, not a duplicate.

Maine's **Public Pre-K** is a universal optional 4-year-old program administered by Maine DOE. The state has been actively expanding Pre-K access. Most districts have a Pre-K Coordinator or run preschool through Special Ed (Part B/619 funding).

Standout dedicated ECE leads: Portland (Kathryn Soucy, Sr Dir of ECE — only true ECE specialist), Biddeford (Rico Mariello, EC Coordinator), RSU 21 (Mary Boswell, Pre-K Coordinator). Larger districts with Curriculum Directors: Bangor (Dr. Marie Robinson), Brunswick (Crofton), Falmouth (Kelly), Yarmouth (Shanna Crofton — 2025 ME Curriculum Leader of the Year), MSAD 51 (Mar-E Trebilcock), RSU 5 (Erica Mazzeo), Gorham (Heather Perry), Scarborough (Katie Vetro), Kittery (Jill Pinard), RSU 14 (Christine Hesler), RSU 22 (Nickerson), Saco (Parkhurst), Auburn (Mogul), RSU 35 (Heidi Early-Hersey).

For most ME districts under ~1,500 students, the Superintendent is the right ECE persona. Special Education Director is a common alternative — preschool is often housed under SpEd because Part B/619 federal funds support it.

Email convention: most districts use branded `.org` (`portlandschools.org`, `bangorschools.net`, `lewistonpublicschools.org`, `rsu21.net`, `msad1.org`). Many small districts use `*.k12.me.us` (legacy) or shared AOS-hosted domains.
Maine DOE, Maine Roads to Quality, and community Head Start agencies (Penquis CAP, KVCAP, YMCA partners) are NOT district staff and are excluded.
Tribal schools (Indian Island, Pleasant Point, Indian Township) are BIE-funded under Maine Indian Education — separate federal structure.

Active 2025-26 leadership transitions to verify before outreach: South Portland, Bangor, Yarmouth, SU 69, SU 76, RSU 84/MSAD 14 (Margaret White resigned Sept 2025), MSAD 28/Five Town (Leigh Anne Neal starts July 2026).
Buying signal: Maine's 2026-27 4-year-old Special Ed transition (CDS-to-SAU) — Veazie is a Cohort 1 district; this is a strong outreach hook for ECE/SpEd Directors statewide.

Plantations and unorganized territory entries (Glenwood Plt, Highland Plt, Upton, Woodville, Lowell, Pleasant Ridge Plt, etc.) often have no operating schools — flagged as not_found and likely not viable for outreach.
